text: The laparoscopic view shows the excision of an endometriosis nodule located on the rectum. The surgeon carefully manipulates instruments to remove the visible mass within the rectovaginal septum.

The surgeon observes complete obliteration of the posterior cul-de-sac upon entry. Filmy adhesions tethering the sigmoid colon to the left pelvic side wall are carefully dissected and removed, allowing for cranial mobilization of the sigmoid. This maneuver reveals the ureter at the level of the pelvic brim.

The surgeon carries the dissection into the pelvis, revealing the ureter at the level of the tethered left ovary. Sharp dissection is used to separate the left ovary from the underlying rectum, followed by the dissection of the rectum off the posterior uterus. The left ovary is bluntly mobilized out of the posterior cul-de-sac and temporarily sutured to the round ligament to keep it out of the surgical field.

The surgeon bluntly mobilizes the right ovary off the pelvic sidewall. The right ureter is identified transperitoneally, and the retroperitoneum is entered just below the ureter to facilitate its complete dissection on the right pelvic sidewall.

The surgeon uses sharp dissection to carefully detach rectal attachments from the posterior uterus, freeing the first attachment for improved visualization. The dissection continues laterally to release the nodule from attachments on the side walls. The nodule is meticulously dissected off the posterior uterus using electrosurgery at the interface, with constant reevaluation of the surroundings to prevent injury.

The surgeon further mobilizes the endometriosis mass off the right pelvic side wall, using a hand placed in the vagina to delineate healthy vaginal tissue from the nodule. The right ovary is temporarily tethered to keep it out of the surgical field.

The surgeon carefully removes the peritoneum overlying the right pelvic sidewall, previously tethered to the ovary, while avoiding the ureter. A rectal probe is placed to assist in the procedure. The endometriosis nodule is sequentially detached from its lateral attachments and then freed from its attachments to the rectum.

The surgeon carefully grasps the residual nodule with a tenaculum and dissects it off the rectum using cold scissors, exposing a friable area of the muscularis layer. This area is oversewn in multiple layers with a barbed PDO suture, ensuring the rectal lumen is not narrowed. An EEA sizer is passed through the rectum to test the lumen after the first layer of suturing.
The surgeon excises the peritoneum overlying the left pelvic side wall, completing the excision process. A rectal leak test is performed to ensure the integrity of the rectum following the procedure.
The patient was discharged home post-op day zero, had a routine post-op course, and reported significantly improved pain at her post-op visit. All specimens removed were notable for endometriosis.
